title: NOC 94142 – Fish and seafood plant workers | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s seafood industry is a global export leader, with a heightened focus on high-value processing and sustainable aquaculture. Fish and Seafood Plant Workers (NOC 94142) are the technical heart of this sector, operating the machinery that cleans, cuts, and packages Canada's premium maritime products. While often seasonal, this role is currently facing a Strong Risk of Shortage across the Atlantic provinces and British Columbia. For international workers, this NOC is a prime entry point into the Canadian labor market, offering high success rates in specialized coastal and rural immigration pilots.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group941 – Processing and manufacturing machine operators

common_job_titles: Seafood Preparer – Fish and Seafood Processing
duties: Machine Operation: Setting up and operating machines to clean, cut, cook, smoke, or dehydrate fish and seafood products.
Packaging: Operating automated machinery to can, bag, or box seafood according to global export standards.
Manual Processing: Cutting, cleaning, and trimming fish using knives or saws; removing meat from crustaceans like lobsters and crabs.
Quality Assurance: Checking products for defects and ensuring conformance to strict Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA) health standards.
2026 Update: Utilizing handheld digital scanners to track "Catch-to-Consumer" traceability data for sustainability certifications.
employment_requirements: Education: Some secondary school education may be required.
Training: Several weeks of on-the-job training are provided by the employer.
Physical Fitness: Ability to stand for long durations and work in cool, damp, and sometimes noisy environments.
Language: CLB 4 is generally sufficient for the Atlantic Immigration Program and most provincial streams.
Safety: Mandatory 2026 WHMIS and site-specific food safety certifications.
salary_prospects: Note: Many rural employers provide subsidized housing or transportation as part of the employment package.
job_outlook: The national projection indicates a "Shortage" environment. With thousands of retirements expected in the next 10 years and the expansion of land-based aquaculture, the seafood processing sector is projected to have more job openings than qualified local job seekers through 2033.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is characterized by a "Critical Labour Gap," with employers heavily relying on international recruits to meet export targets:
Nova Scotia & New Brunswick: Very Good prospects. Chronic shortages persist in rural coastal communities. Demand is driven by the lobster, crab, and salmon processing peaks.
Newfoundland and Labrador: Strong demand. Essential for the processing of groundfish and shrimp. Employers often offer additional incentives for workers in remote regions.
British Columbia: Good prospects. Focused on high-tech salmon and shellfish processing in the Lower Mainland and Vancouver Island.
Prince Edward Island: Stable to Good. High seasonal demand for shellfish and finfish processors.
National Trend: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) identifies this NOC as a "Strong Risk of Shortage" through 2033, largely due to an aging workforce (55% are over age 50).
Why Work in Canada as a Seafood Plant Worker?
Low Barrier to Entry: No formal post-secondary degree is required; most training happens on-the-job, allowing for immediate employment.
Technological Shift: Transition from manual cutting to operating 2026-grade automated filleting machines and digital weighing/scanning systems.
Path to PR: Frequently prioritized in the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) and the Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RCIP).
Flexible Schedules: While intense during peak seasons, many roles offer significant overtime pay and the ability to travel within Canada during off-seasons.
pathways: Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): This is the premier pathway for seafood plant workers. It allows employers in Atlantic Canada to hire international talent and provide a direct route to Permanent Residency.
Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RCIP): A high-success option for those working in regional communities that rely on the blue economy.
Quebec Food Processing Pilot (Until Jan 2026): For those working in Quebec, this pilot offers a dedicated PR pathway for seafood processing workers.
TR to PR Pathway (2026): Seafood plant workers are classified as "Essential Workers," making them eligible for this 33,000-person transition quota.
